coredata.dc:title: Emission enhancement of fluorescent glutathione-capped gold nanoclusters by cerium (III) ion-induced aggregation for sensitive detecting α-glucosidase in human serum using ratiometric fluorometry
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Wei
Jiang, Xue
Wu, Yanhua
Jiang, Jing
Liu, Xin
Liu, Yibing
Wang, Wei
Lai, Jinyu
Wang, Xinghua
coredata.dc:description: A fluorescence probe was designed based on carbon dots (CDs) and glutathione-capped gold nanoclusters (GSH-AuNCs) for detecting α-glucosidase in human serum. By employing l-ascorbic acid-2-O-α-d-glucopyranosyl (AAG) as substrate for the α-glucosidase catalyzed hydrolysis reaction, ascorbic acid (AA) was generated, and subsequently reduced cerium (IV) ion (Ce4+) to Ce3+. Since Ce3+ would selectively improve the aggregation of GSH-AuNCs, the consequent aggregation-induced emission enhancement (AIEE) of aggregated GSH-AuNCs was measured for indicating the activity of α-glucosidase involved in the hydrolysis process. To furtherly improve the precision of spectral response, ratiometric fluorometry was realized by taking the fluorescence emission of CDs, which was affected insignificantly during the above process, as the fluorescence reference. The developed probe exhibited a linear response to α-glucosidase activity in the range of 0.01 ∼ 0.1 U‧mL−1, with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.0055 U‧mL−1. Results of serum sample examination and cellular imaging reveal the promising potential of the developed probe for clinical diagnosis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Glutathione-capped gold nanoclusters
Carbon dots
Ratiometric fluorometry
α-Glucosidase
Cellular imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparations of fluorescence CDs and GSH-AuNCs
Detections of α-glucosidase and its inhibitor
Optical properties of CDs and GSH-AuNCs
Detection of α-glucosidase in real sample
Cytotoxicity assay and bioimaging of α-glucosidase in living cells

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image and particle size distribution of CDs (A) and GSH-AuNCs (B). FT-IR spectra of CDs (C) and GSH-AuNCs (D).

Spectra of fluorescence emission of the testing system (A). Effect of Ce3+ concentration on fluorescence emission of GSH-AuNCs (B). Fluorescence emission decay curves of GSH-AuNCs in absence and presence of Ce3+ (C). Effect of Ce3+ concentration on RLS of GSH-AuNC (D). Concentrations: Ce3+, 10 μM; Ce4+, 30 μM; AAG, 30 μM. Environmental pH level, 7.4. Fluorescence excitation wavelength, 350 nm.

Fluorescence spectra of testing systems with different α-glucosidase activities (A) and DNJ concentrations (B). Plot of fluorescence response signals against activities of α-glucosidase (C). Plot of inhibition efficiency against concentrations of DNJ (D). Initial activity of α-glucosidase for inhibition measurements was set at 0.1 U‧mL−1.

Confocal fluorescence images of HepG2 cells. Cells only (a). Cells treated with CDs and GSH-AuNCs for 2 h (b). Cells treated with CDs and GSH-AuNCs for 2 h, followed with Ce4+ and AAG and α-glucosidase for 40 min (c). Cells treated with CDs and GSH-AuNCs for 2 h, followed with Ce4+, AAG, α-glucosidase and DNJ for 40 min (d). Excitation wavelength was set as 350 nm. Wavelength ranges of blue channel and red channel were 400 nm ∼ 550 nm and 550 nm ∼ 750 nm, respectively.
Schematic diagram for detection of α-glucosidase.
Results of detecting α-glucosidase in human serum samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Emission enhancement of fluorescent glutathione-capped gold nanoclusters by cerium (III) ion-induced aggregation for sensitive detecting α-glucosidase in human serum using ratiometric fluorometry

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Qianjin Street 2699, Changchun 130012, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Qianjin Street 2699, Changchun 130012, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for Molecular Enzymology and Engineering of the Ministry of Education, School of Life Sciencesm Jilin University, Changchun 130012, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for Molecular Enzymology and Engineering of the Ministry of Education

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory for Molecular Enzymology and Engineering of the Ministry of Education, School of Life Sciencesm Jilin University, Changchun 130012, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Division of Clinical Research, The First Hospital of Jilin University, Changchun 130021, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The First Hospital of Jilin University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Division of Clinical Research, The First Hospital of Jilin University, Changchun 130021, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A fluorescence probe was designed based on carbon dots (CDs) and glutathione-capped gold nanoclusters (GSH-AuNCs) for detecting α-glucosidase in human serum. By employing l-ascorbic acid-2-O-α-d-glucopyranosyl (AAG) as substrate for the α-glucosidase catalyzed hydrolysis reaction, ascorbic acid (AA) was generated, and subsequently reduced cerium (IV) ion (Ce4+) to Ce3+. Since Ce3+ would selectively improve the aggregation of GSH-AuNCs, the consequent aggregation-induced emission enhancement (AIEE) of aggregated GSH-AuNCs was measured for indicating the activity of α-glucosidase involved in the hydrolysis process. To furtherly improve the precision of spectral response, ratiometric fluorometry was realized by taking the fluorescence emission of CDs, which was affected insignificantly during the above process, as the fluorescence reference. The developed probe exhibited a linear response to α-glucosidase activity in the range of 0.01 ∼ 0.1 U‧mL−1, with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.0055 U‧mL−1. Results of serum sample examination and cellular imaging reveal the promising potential of the developed probe for clinical diagnosis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Diabetes mellitus is a group of metabolic disorders of human body with a characteristic of chronic hyperglycemia [1]. α-Glucosidase as a membrane-binding enzyme in small intestinal epithelium, not only participates in the metabolism of sugar in the organism, but also plays an important role in maintaining normal physiological functions of human body [2]. High level of α-glucosidase will promote excess glucose production in plasma, leading to hyperglycemia, diabetes and their complications [3]. α-Glucosidase is also one of the most important targets in the treatment of Type II diabetes. Thus, quantitative detection of α-glucosidase is crucial in clinical diagnosis and pharmacodynamics assessment.

Recently, many sensing systems have been designed and applied for α-glucosidase activity monitoring, including electrochemisty [4], high-performance liquid chromatography [5], colorimetry [6], and fluorometry [7,8]. Among these methods, fluorometry has attracted much attention due to its advantages of both high sensitivity and convenient detection [6,9].

Carbon dots (CDs) as fluorescent indicators and probes have been widely applied in chemical and biological analysis due to their unique optical and electronic properties [10–13]. Metal nanoclusters protected by glutathione exhibiting the characteristic of tunable aggregation-induced emission enhancement (AIEE) provide more possibilities for constructing fluorescent probes [14–17]. Especially, the AIEE of glutathione-capped gold nanoclusters (GSH-AuNCs) can be modulated by organic solvents [18] and metal ions [19]. Such changes of the luminescent property were closely related to the restriction or promotion of inter- and intramolecular motions of the molecules modified on the surface of aggregated or dispersive GSH-AuNCs, since these motions facilitate the non-radiative energy transfer for the decay of excited states of GSH-AuNCs [20].

In this work, we constructed a ratiometric fluorescence probe (Scheme 1 ) based on CDs and GSH-AuNCs for sensitive detecting α-glucosidase in human serum. In the presence of α-glucosidase, l-ascorbic acid-2-O-α-d-glucopyranosyl (AAG), the substrate for hydrolysis reaction catalyzed by α-glucosidase, can be specifically hydrolyzed to ascorbic acid (AA). Then, Ce3+ ions generated during the reduction of Ce4+ by AA consequently induce the AIEE effect of GSH-AuNCs. Therefore, along with the increasing activities of α-glucosidase, the fluorescence emission of GSH-AuNCs at 650 nm increase gradually. To furtherly reduce the fluctuations in acquired fluorescence signals, nitrogen and oxygen co-doped CDs with a maximum emission at 470 nm were prepared by hydrothermal method and served as the fluorescence reference for calibration. Compared with other analytical methods for detecting α-glucosidase based on fluorometry, a novel responding mechanism based on AIEE of GSH-AuNCs as well as a ratiometric calibration using CDs as fluorescence reference were proposed and applied in this work. The present probe was applied to the determination of α-glucosidase in human serum samples and the fluorescence imaging of live cells and demonstrates considerable potential for practical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparations of fluorescence CDs and GSH-AuNCs
ce:section.ce:para: CDs were prepared by a reported hydrothermal method with minor modifications [21]. Briefly, 0.5 g of citric acid, 0.25 mL of ethylenediamine and 0.01 g of calcein were dissolved in 10 mL of ultrapure water. Then, the mixed solution was transferred into a 30-mL Teflon-lined autoclaved, followed by heating at 150 °C for 2 h. The resultant was filtered through a 0.22-μm microporous membrane, and the filtrate was collected and stored at 4 °C for further use.

GSH-AuNCs were prepared according to a previous literature [22]. Freshly prepared aqueous solutions of HAuCl4 (200 mM, 1 mL) and glutathione (100 mM, 0.4 mL) were mixed with 8.6 mL of ultrapure water, followed by vigorous stirring at room temperature until the mixed solution reached colorless. Then, the mixed solution was heated at 70 °C under gentle stirring for 24 h. Finally, the product was collected and stored at 4 °C in dark for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detections of α-glucosidase and its inhibitor
ce:section.ce:para: For detecting α-glucosidase, 30 μL of AAG (1 mM) and 100 μL of α-glucosidase were successively introduced into 640 μL of Tris-HCl buffer (10 mM, pH = 7.4). The mixed solution was incubated at 37 °C for 40 min, followed by addition of 30 μL of Ce4+ (1 mM). After reaction for 2 min, 100 μL of CDs and 100 μL of GSH-AuNCs were added into the above solution. Finally, the resulting solution was tested with a fluorophotometer at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm. The intensity ratio of fluorescence emission at 470 nm against that at 650 nm (F 470/F 650) was calculated as the response signal.

To verify the dynamic response of the present probe towards the activity of α-glucosidase, an inhibitor of α-glucosidase, 1-Deoxynojirimycin (DNJ), was employed to obtain gradient activity changes of α-glucosidase in testing systems. Briefly, 30 μL of AAG (1 mM), 100 μL of α-glucosidase (1 U‧mL−1), and 100 μL of DNJ were successively introduced into 540 μL of Tris-HCl buffer (10 mM, pH = 7.4). After that, a similar measurement procedure as mentioned above was performed to assess the inhibition ability of DNJ.

Procedures of MTT colorimetric assays for evaluating cellular cytotoxicity of CDs and GSH-AuNCs, and fluorescence imaging of the distribution of α-glucosidase in living cells were described in the Supplementary Material.

Spiked samples were prepared by adding α-glucosidase into three human serum samples from individual healthy volunteers provided by the First Hospital of Jilin University (Changchun, China). The detection procedures were similar to these described in section 2.2.

Morphologies of CDs and GSH-AuNCs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Fig. 1 ). Both of CDs and GSH-AuNCs display a quasi-spherical shape. As shown in Fig. 1A and B, diameters of CDs and GSH-AuNCs were distributed 0.5 nm ∼ 3.5 nm and 1.1 ∼ 2.1 nm, with average values of 2.0 nm and 1.5 nm, respectively.

Furtherly, identifications of surface chemical groups and elemental constitutions of CDs and GSH-AuNCs were performed by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). In FT-IR spectrum of CDs (Fig. 1C), the broad absorption band at 3424 cm−1 is associated with the OH and NH stretching vibrations, the band at 2978 cm−1 is attributed to CH stretching vibration, the bands at 1560 cm−1 and 1263 cm−1 are assigned to the bending vibrations of NH and CN [23,24], the bands at 1381 cm−1 and 1056 cm−1 emerge from bending vibrations of COH and OH [25], respectively. The XPS spectra (Fig. S1) reveal the elemental composition of CDs. Three main peaks emerged at 284.6 eV, 399.8 eV and 532.0 eV in the full scan survey spectrum (Fig. S1A) are correspond to C 1s (69.06 %), N 1s (17.02 %) and O 1s (13.92 %), respectively [26]. High-resolution spectra of C 1s (Fig. S1C) display-three peaks at 284.7 eV, 286.4 eV and 287.9 eV, which are respectively derived from the CC/CC, CO, and CN/CO groups. Two peaks at 399.7 eV and 401.1 eV in the high-resolution spectrum of N 1s (Fig. S1D) are respectively ascribed to CNC and NH groups [27]. Groups containing O element were identified as CO and COC/COH, with the characteristic peaks at 531.8 eV and 532.7 eV, respectively.

The FT-IR spectrum of the GSH-AuNCs was shown in Fig. 1D. The broad absorption band at 3029 cm−1 is associated with the OH and NH stretching vibrations. The bands at 1727 cm−1 and 1650 cm−1 are respectively corresponding to the stretching vibrations of CO and CN groups [28]. The bands at 1538 cm−1, 1410 cm−1 and 1218 cm−1 are assigned to the bending vibrations of NH, COH and CN. Moreover, results of XPS measurement indicate the existence of C, N, O, S, Cl and Au in GSH-AuNCs (Fig. S1B). Two peaks of Au 4f (Fig. S1F) in GSH-AuNCs at 88.3 eV and 84.6 eV correspond to Au 4f5/2 and Au 4f7/2 [29], respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of CDs and GSH-AuNCs
ce:section.ce:para: Optical properties of CDs and GSH-AuNCs were studied by ultraviolet–visible absorption (UV–vis) and fluorescence spectroscopies. As displayed in Fig. S2A, two obvious absorption bands emerged at 240 nm and 350 nm, which can be attributed to the π-π* and n-π* transitions of acyclic alkene or carbonyl groups (CC/CO) and amine moiety present in the CDs [24]. Similarly, two excitation peaks at 245 nm and 367 nm indicate that there are two types of excitation energy-trapping modes of the CDs, which is consistent with the information revealed by the above UV–vis absorption spectrum. Fig. S3A shows the fluorescent emission spectra of the CDs at various excitation wavelengths between 300 nm and 400 nm, demonstrating a very slight excitation-dependent photoluminescence behavior of the CDs. As illustrated in Fig. S2B, the GSH-AuNCs exhibits a broad absorption band, and shows maximum excitation and emission at 275 nm and 650 nm, respectively. Fig. S3B shows the fluorescent emission spectra of the GSH-AuNCs at various excitation wavelengths between 230 nm and 370 nm, demonstrating the excitation-independent photoluminescence behavior of GSH-AuNCs. The fluorescence quantum yields (QYs) of CDs and GSH-AuNCs were evaluated to be 62.30 % and 4.86 % by an absolute method, respectively.

For obtaining superior analytical performance, effects of experimental conditions including the concentration of Ce4+ and AAG, the environmental pH and the incubation time were investigated. As shown in Fig. S4A and S4B, 30 μM Ce4+ and 30 μM AAG were chosen as the optimum concentrations. According to the results shown in Fig. S4C, Tris-HCl buffer (pH = 7.4) was selected to maintain the environmental pH level of testing systems for following experiments, which is also consistent with the normal pH of human blood. The incubation time prior to analysis was also optimized, results (Fig. S4D) suggested an incubation time of 40 min was sufficient.

The response mechanism of this probe was studied by fluorescence emission spectroscopy (Fig. 2 A), fluorescence decay profile (Fig. 2C) and the resonance light scattering (RLS) spectroscopy (Fig. 2D). As presented in Fig. 2A, after all the above reagents were added into the testing system, the fluorescence emission of GSH-AuNCs was enhanced significantly, while the fluorescence emission of CDs changed very little. According to previous reports, Ce3+ can trigger the aggregation of GSH-AuNCs owing to the strong affinity between Ce3+ and carboxylate groups of the glutathione ligands [22], which restrains both the inter- and the intramolecular vibrational motions of the chemical groups on the surface of GSH-AuNCs and results in the inhibiting non-radiative relaxation of the excited GSH-AuNCs [30,31]. In the above testing system, AA was generated during the hydrolysis of AAG by α-glucosidase, and, consequently reduced Ce4+ to Ce3+, which would induce the AIEE of GSH-AuNCs. Thus, increasing α-glucosidase activities lead to increasing fluorescence emission of GSH-AuNCs.

Moreover, the fluorescence lifetime of GSH-AuNCs increased after introducing Ce3+ to the testing system (Fig. 2C), indicating fluorescence emission enhance as well as the aggregation of the GSH-AuNCs [32]. RLS is proved effective for investigating aggregated systems, since aggregation of particles will lead to enhanced RLS signal [33]. RLS spectra of the testing system with different concentrations of Ce3+ shown in Fig. 2D reveal the aggregation of GSH-AuNCs was promoted by increasing Ce3+ concentrations. The above results proved the AIEE process of GSH-AuNCs induced by Ce3+, which makes the fluorescence assay of α-glucosidase possible.

To evaluate the selectivity of the present probe towards α-glucosidase in complicated biological media, the influence of ions and biological substances commonly coexisting in human serum on the response signals were investigated. Results illustrated in Fig. S5 reveal that no obvious interference from the above ions or substances were observed, indicating the good selectivity of this probe.

Fig. 3 demonstrates the fluorescence responses of the probe towards α-glucosidase in absence and presence of DNJ. As shown in Fig. 3A, along with the increasing activities of α-glucosidase, the fluorescence emission of GSH-AuNCs at 650 nm increased gradually. By using the fluorescence emission of CDs at 470 nm as a reference signal, a good linear relationship was obtained between the values of fluorescence intensity ratio F 650/F 470 and α-glucosidase activity ranging from 0.01 U‧mL−1 to 0.1 U‧mL−1 (Fig. 3C). The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 0.0055 U‧mL−1 (S/N = 3). The above results demonstrate that the analytical performance of this probe for α-glucosidase detection is superior or comparable to many previously reported methods (Table S1).

DNJ is a common inhibitor of α-glucosidase for suppressing the elevation of postprandial hyperglycemia [34], which can penetrate cells rapidly to potently inhibit a-glucosidase in a competitive manner. Ability of the proposed probe to screen DNJ (Fig. 3B) was investigated. Inhibition efficiency (IE, %) of DNJ was calculated by the following equation:
IE
( % )
=
F
no
i n h i b i t o r
-
F
inhibitor
/
F
no
i n h i b i t o r
-
F 0
× 100 %
where F inhibitor, F no inhibitor and F0 represent the values of fluorescence intensity ratio F 650/F 470 obtained by measuring the testing systems in presence of DNJ, in absence of DNJ, and in absence of both α-glucosidase and DNJ, respectively. The relationship between inhibition efficiency and DNJ concentration is shown in Fig. 3D. The concentration of DNJ where the fluorescence response of this probe was reduced by half, IC50 , was calculated to be 14.9 μM, which is well accorded with the reported values obtained by other methods [35]. The above results demonstrate this present sensing strategy possessed great potential in analysis and screening of α-glucosidase inhibitor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of α-glucosidase in real sample
ce:section.ce:para: Real human serum samples spiked with α-glucosidase were examined for evaluating the practicability of the present probe (Table 1 ). The recoveries ranged from 97.5 % to 106.0 %, and the relative deviations (RSDs) were less than 3.52 %. The good accordance between results obtained by our method and by reference colorimetric method [36] demonstrates the promising potential of the present probe for detection of α-glucosidase in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity assay and bioimaging of α-glucosidase in living cells
ce:section.ce:para: The overall cellular cytotoxicity of reagents employed or generated during sensing is dominant for biological applications of a bioprobe. As shown in Fig. S6, the viabilities of HepG2 cells were still higher than 75 % after individually incubated with CDs and GSH-AuNCs for 24 h, indicating the low cell toxicity of the probe.

This probe was furtherly used in fluorescence imaging of intracellular α-glucosidase. As shown in Fig. 4 a, blank HepG2 cells (control, cells only) exhibit no obvious fluorescence. Cells incubated with CDs and GSH-AuNCs for 2 h exhibited bright blue fluorescence and relatively weak red fluorescence (Fig. 4b). Upon the addition of α-glucosidase, fluorescence of the red channel was enhanced (Fig. 4c). After being treated with DNJ, fluorescence signal from the red channel decreased (Fig. 4d). Fluorescence from the blue channel varies very little during the above treatments. Above results reveal that the present probe possesses good cell membrane permeability and is suitable for fluorescence imaging of α-glucosidase in cells.

In summary, we proposed a novel probe based on nitrogen and oxygen co-doped CDs and GSH-AuNCs for highly sensitive determination of α-glucosidase by ratiometric fluorometry. AAG was employed as the substrate for hydrolysis catalyzed by α-glucosidase to generate AA. AA can reduce Ce4+ to Ce3+, and subsequently the binding between Ce3+ and carboxyl groups on surface of GSH-AuNCs would induce AIEE effect of GSH-AuNCs. Therefore, the fluorescence emission of GSH-AuNCs at 650 nm will be enhanced along with the increasing activity of α-glucosidase in testing system. By using the fluorescence emission of CDs at 470 nm as reference, on which the influence of the above reactions was negligible, activity of α-glucosidase in the testing system was indicated by the fluorescence intensity ratio F 650/F 470, and the fluctuations in acquired fluorescence signals were eliminated. The present probe exhibits high reliability and good practicability for detecting α-glucosidase in human serum and cellular imaging, demonstrates its promising potential for future applications in bioanalysis and clinical diagnosis.
All applicable international, national, and/or institutional guidelines for the collection and use of human blood and serum samples were followed.
The study was approved by the Institutional Ethics Committee of China-Japan Union Hospital of Jilin University, Changchun, China (No. 2019040811).

Wei Zhang: Methodology, Writing – original draft, Data curation, Investigation. Xue Jiang: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis. Yanhua Wu: Writing – original draft, Investigation. Jing Jiang: Methodology, Project administration, Writing – review & editing. Xin Liu: Project administration, Writing – review & editing. Yibing Liu: Project administration, Writing – review & editing. Wei Wang: Project administration, Writing – review & editing. Jinyu Lai: Project administration, Writing – review & editing. Xinghua Wang: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities of China, the Research Project of Education Department of Jilin Province (No. JJKH20200944KJ), and the Training Program of Excellent Young Teachers of Jilin University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2022.108049.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
